metadata.title:
PAYS MONEY TO


HIRELINGS in COURT

pages.raw_text:
Judge Keunan today denied the motion for stay of
proceedings in the application of W. W. Hindman for
writ of mandate to compel the city council to submit to
the people the charter amendment providing for refer
endum of franchises The judge took the ground
taken by Judge Warren Saturday night that stay of
proceedings at this time would nullfy the writ.
Although Judge Warren made this ruling Saturday
night after overrung the motion to quas overriding the
defendants and the half dozen other objections
raised by the corporation attorneys these lawyers sought
to carry the matter before Judge KEENAN, on regular
motion to stay the proceedings pending appeal to the
supreme court.


Judge KEENAN, after listening to the arguments of Cor
poraton Counsel Dudley and the lawyers representing
the Washington Water Power company the gas companies
ana the old telephone company announced that he had
previously considered the question raised application
for writ of mandate and had overruled The case had
gone to the supreme court which had sustained the ruling


Immediately after the decision was announced Mr.
Dudley filed regular notice of appeal to the supreme
|court.


After the proceedings had closed there ensued
which is rarely witnessed in court room in connection
with


The old telephone company, through its representative
Charles Reynolds, had called many witnesses presumably


for the purpose OF attacking the genuneness of the sigma
lures to the petition These witnesses were not placed on
the stand.


But Agent Reynolds, court over took up
his position at table and placed thereon big pill Of
money Then the "workngmen" and other witnesses
who to have testified for the corporations filed by
the table gave their names and paid for their time
and trouble in appearing in court.


The money paid openly and with attempt to dis
guse the fact that the witnesses were brought there by
the corporations and at agreed price.


1t was stated by of the attorneys afterwards that
the corporations had summoned several hundreds wit
nesses all of whom told that they would be paid for
their time by the companies concerned
